author: @danchadwick1495
text: I got a mobile for my child that this reminds me of... Placed above a small light bulb, it spun a moving picture on the walls, and made it appear the room was moving. This video subject is easily moved by the slightest breeze, what if it were to be placed at the top of a solar panel? Or a cylinder version across the top, extracting a few more percent from the solar radiation.

author: @Barbreck1
text:
Great stuff Robert.  I have often wondered why no-one has considered combining hydraulic accumulators with wind-turbines to build a free-energy system with energy stored as hydro-pneumatic pressure.  
If, rather than having the turbine driving an alternator, you instead drive a hydraulic pump (perhaps a radial piston or screw pump), as these require very little relative rotary effort to generate very high pressures at perfectly adequate flow-rates.  You can have several turbines commonly piped to a hydraulic motor driving a much heavier alternator on the ground.  The hydraulic system can be designed to drive the hydraulic motor (and alternator) at moderate wind speeds (by regulating the flow to the motor).  When the wind turbines produce excess flow (by driving the pumps faster), this can be drawn off as increased pressure (via a pressure relief valve) to a (very large) hydro-pneumatic accumulator (essentially a big hydraulic cylinder where gas is trapped in the upper chamber- to act as a pneumatic spring).  The excess energy produced by the system will drive the piston up the cylinder, compressing the air above and creating a reservoir of pressurised gas.  When the wind stops blowing, the gas pressure will drive the piston down again, pushing the hydraulic oil back through the system to the motor and providing driving energy to keep the alternator turning until the wind picks up again.
Obviously, the accumulator required for a large turbine would be sizable, although I have thought it could be incorporated into the base of the turbine pylons.  Nonetheless, the concept could be proven out in the lab with a scaled down version.

author: @InterdimensionalWiz
text:
Hey Robert...  a sturmey archer bike dynamo in a bike wheel... front wheel with NO GEARS... gives about 6-10 watts... AC... so a Voltage doubler can be used for 12 VOLTS OUTPUT! got 20 volts once! about 0.4 amp (goes faster than on a bike!)..... circle of aluminium from a caravan or tin from the side of a washing machine etc....size of the wheel, 16 blades! keep the center of the circle, a big fan bolted to the RIM. bend 'em up,  goes ALL the time.
ALSO... a bottle dynamo can be used but needs much higher RPM... 3 slender blades, each about 10 inches long.... bent aliminium... and BINGO.... loads of flashing LEDs AC again so voltage doubler if you like!.... or a cascade multiplier and get as much volts as you like.  :o) wrote a booklet on it once.
had aver 100 people send me pictures of ones they built from the bike wheel. dyna hub, charges a cordless drill, ran a synth... all sorts.... all metal lasts for years... 2 pieces of angle iron bolted or welded together ,to mount fan and tail and a 6 inch bolt facing down as a swivel to fit in a tent pole or water pipe as the pole.... or wooden pole with a foot of pipe atached.... dont forget some steel washing line and some really long tent pegs!!.... no no..... really long!  or screw it to your shed.

author: @rowgler1
text: I think that cloth sail wind generators should be given a second look. For thousands of years masts and sails transported ships across the oceans. Now we have access to better fabrics and stronger, lighter materials for struts and masts. Put that old technology into use with modern bearings and auto furling systems and it would be relatively easy to build a practical homestead power source. I really like the way you think.

author: @PressToDigitate
text: That really is cute.  If you mated that with a (DiY) battery that could charge a phone (even if it took days of wind to do so), and packaged the lot of it as a school project *in kits* , replete with lesson plans on the aerodynamics, the dynamo conversion from motor to generator, and the battery chemistry/materials science, you could probably *sell such kits in bunches* for classroom use.  Your teaching videos would sell it without even "selling" because you're such an excellent teacher and presenter on these things.
